topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of old paper and lukewarm coffee clung to the air in the study, a familiar, suffocating blanket. I found it tucked beneath a stack of annotated scripts—a manuscript. My partner, Elias, had been spending hours hunched over his desk, the lamplight catching the determined set of his jaw. It wasn't a business proposal or a draft of a screenplay; it was a novel. And the protagonist, the central, painfully detailed figure, was undeniably me. A collection of my awkward silences, my obsessive need for order, the way I meticulously organized my anxieties into neat, fragile boxes. I picked it up, the weight of the pages settling heavily in my hands. I didn't move to tear it out. I just sat down in the armchair opposite him, the silence stretching between us until it felt taut, like a wire about to snap. I looked at Elias, whose eyes were fixed on the page, a faint, almost beatific smile playing on his lips. I simply nodded, a small, almost imperceptible movement, and picked up my mug, the ceramic warm against my palms.

[story 2]

It happened during a routine tidy-up of the shared bookshelf. Behind a volume of obscure poetry, I found a thick, leather-bound book, clearly unfinished. The title page was blank, but the handwriting inside the margins was Elias’s—intricate, flowing, and undeniably personal. I flipped through the pages, and there, meticulously chronicled in prose that mirrored my own internal monologue, was my life. Every fleeting thought, every misplaced emotion, every mundane Tuesday evening spent staring out the window—it was all there, rendered with a startling, almost clinical accuracy. A strange flatness settled over me, a kind of dull ache behind my ribs. I closed the book slowly, placing it back exactly where I found it, smoothing the spine as if preparing it for storage. When Elias walked in, humming tunelessly, I didn't look up immediately. I just continued rearranging the books, my movements slow, deliberate, devoid of any sudden energy. "Found something interesting?" he asked, his tone light, entirely missing the gravity radiating off me. I offered a small shrug, my gaze drifting toward the windowpane, where the afternoon light seemed unusually muted.

[story 3]

I walked into the living room late one evening, drawn by the faint, rhythmic scratching sound coming from Elias’s room. Curiosity, a sensation I rarely indulged anymore, pulled me toward the door. I paused just outside, listening. It was the sound of a pen moving across heavy stock paper. When I finally pushed the door open, the scene was exactly as I had imagined: Elias, bathed in the soft glow of his desk lamp, utterly absorbed. The manuscript lay open, and the words flowed, weaving a narrative so intimately detailed it felt like reading my own diary from a vantage point I couldn't escape. A profound weariness washed over me, heavy and encompassing. I didn't speak. I simply leaned against the doorframe, observing the tableau—my partner, transforming my existence into narrative material. I felt a strange stillness settle over my limbs, a complete lack of impulse to intervene, to question, or to react. I just stood there, letting the quiet observation consume me until I felt heavy enough to sink into the floorboards.
